title: The Waiting Is the Point: Time, Suffering, and Medicaid
description:
“To be poor and sick in America is to live in delay. The Medicaid system does not just reflect that reality—it enforces it.”

The post The Waiting Is the Point: Time, Suffering, and Medicaid appeared first on Public Books.
hook: Medicaid doesn't just make poor Americans wait—waiting is the entire point, a tool of control disguised as bureaucracy.

title: Lay It Down
description: "People love John Samson Fellows’s music. He doesn’t want to make it anymore."
hook: A musician beloved by his audience decides he's done making music for them. What happens when success becomes unbearable?

title: Her mother murdered her father in an infamous case. Now, she's telling her own story
description: Lucille Miller was convicted of killing her husband in 1965. Now her daughter Debra reflects on her own traumatic childhood and its lingering effects in The Most Wonderful Terrible Person.
hook: She grew up with her mother's infamy. Now she's reclaiming the story everyone thought they already knew about her family.

title: How Mormons went mainstream
description: Everything is coming up Mormon…or at least it feels that way. From reality TV drama to cookies to sodas to how we think about femininity, the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints is at the forefront of culture in the US. For a religion that only 2 percent of Americans follow, Mormonism is sure […]
hook: Mormonism is 2% of America but everywhere in culture—cookies, femininity, reality TV. How did a fringe religion become the aesthetic of now?

title: Sunday at La Bombonera
description: “On its biggest days, football comes down to contempt, and nobody hates each other more than Swine hate Lepers.”
hook: In football's most heated matches, the game distills to pure contempt. This is what that looks like from the inside.
